name: European Honeybee
description: The familiar golden-and-black striped bee introduced from Europe. Lives in large hives of 20,000+ workers. Excellent generalist pollinator — visits nearly any flower with accessible nectar and pollen.
notes: Avoid broad-spectrum pesticides even when hive is not nearby — honeybees forage up to 3 miles.
funFact: A single honeybee visits 50–100 flowers per foraging trip.

name: Common Eastern Bumblebee
description: Chunky, fuzzy black-and-yellow bee with a distinctive buzz. Lives in small underground colonies (50–500 bees). Can 'buzz pollinate' — vibrating flowers to release pollen — which is critical for tomatoes, peppers, and eggplants.
funFact: Bumblebees can fly in cooler temperatures than most bees — they warm up by shivering their flight muscles.

name: Eastern Carpenter Bee
description: Very large, black bee with a shiny, hairless abdomen (often confused with bumblebees, which are fuzzy all over). Burrows into unpainted wood to nest. Males are territorial but have no stinger.
notes: Sometimes 'steals' nectar by slitting the side of tubular flowers — the plant still benefits from other visitors.
funFact: Males guard territory by hovering and 'bluff-charging' intruders — but they can't sting.

name: Metallic Green Sweat Bee
description: A jewel of the garden — brilliant metallic green head and thorax (males have a yellow-and-black striped abdomen; females are solid green). Nests in the ground in loose colonies.
funFact: Named 'sweat bees' because some species are attracted to the salt in human perspiration on hot days.

name: Leafcutter Bee
description: Small dark bee with a large head and jaws for cutting perfect circles out of leaves to build her nest cells. Carries pollen on her belly hairs instead of back legs — gives her a golden-tummy look.
notes: The circular holes you see in rose or redbud leaves are usually leafcutter bees — not a pest; a sign of a healthy garden.
funFact: Each nest cell is a tiny green 'cigar' rolled from leaf pieces, stocked with pollen and one egg.

name: Mason Bee
description: Compact, dark bees with a slight metallic blue or green sheen. Named for their habit of sealing nest holes with mud. One of the most efficient pollinators — a single mason bee does the work of ~100 honeybees in an orchard.
notes: Install a mason bee house (6-inch tubes, 5/16 inch diameter) in a warm, east-facing spot to host them.
funFact: Docile — males have no stinger and females only sting if squeezed.

name: Squash Bee
description: A specialist that pollinates only squash, pumpkin, and gourd flowers. Males spend the night inside closed squash blossoms. Often confused for honeybees but stouter and faster.
notes: Active at dawn — most squash flowers close by mid-morning. If you grow squash, you almost certainly have them.
funFact: They evolved alongside squash in the Americas, long before honeybees were introduced.

name: Long-horned Bee
description: Fuzzy tan bees with very long antennae (males, especially). Specialists on sunflower-family flowers. Ground nesters — often in small loose colonies in bare sandy soil.
funFact: Males often cluster together at night, clinging to a plant stem in groups of 5–20 — a charming 'bee sleepover.'

name: Small Carpenter Bee
description: Tiny, shiny dark blue-black bees — often mistaken for flies. Hollow out the pith of dead plant stems to make nests. Very important pollinators of small-flowered plants.
notes: Leave old stems of plants like raspberry canes or sunflower stalks standing 12 inches tall over winter — they'll nest inside.

name: Digger Bee
description: Fast-flying, fuzzy bees that look like small bumblebees but fly with a higher-pitched buzz. Excavate burrows in well-drained soil — you may see small conical mounds in spring lawn edges.
notes: Leave a small patch of bare, well-drained soil somewhere in the garden for them to nest.
funFact: Despite having a nest in the ground, they're docile and very rarely sting.
